speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 21, 1995. We're in Lincolnwood, Illinois, USA. We're speaking with Mr. Harry Toporek. I'm Marsha Blitzstein. This is tape number two. Mr. Toporek, you were talking about the food in the ghetto-- and the fact that the Germans supplied the food. Were there ration cards? How was the food distribution handled?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], I mean like the City Hall in here-- they knew exactly how many Jews we got. And the Germans used to give us a ration. But they gave us non-kosher meat. And I remember that was the beginning and middle of 1941. My mother used to say, you know, they give us meat that's not kosher-- because that was horse meat. They said, I'm going to give you two parts whatever you need for the children. We are old people.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] So this concept-- although you were getting some food, it was very hard specifically for your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I didn't even realize-- that my mother would tell us to eat treif meat. [SOBBING] I didn't realize the danger. She knew. She knew. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. No. So she chose not to eat that as much as she could.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she gave us permission to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: She gave the children--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we should keep the plates separate. You know? We should not mix with the regular because we had kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, that was a frightening experience for us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She asks us to eat treif to survive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were still were three, four family-- three, four members of the family. And you know, I mean, when you look back now, the wisdom that my mother had to ask us that we should eat treif to survive-- Dad didn't eat. Dad didn't touch it. She said that we had the right to live. We are old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you realize that now looking back? Or were you aware of most of this at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was aware. I mean, that is a tragedy that my mother should OK to eat treif. I didn't even realize that-- in which direction we're going. The elderly people-- my mother did realize. She knew. You know, if my father would say that, maybe I can understand. But my mother. And that bothered me so much that my mother gave me the OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That did. So you did realize it. How old was your mother at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was approximately the same age-- 58, 59-- as my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people that were living with you, that were forced to live with you-- was their religious background similar to that of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the same kind of people like we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they handle the situation with the food in a similar way, as far as kosher and non-kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you, I did not-- I think my parents used to help them, whatever they could. Because they didn't have even what we had. And they were also religious people. You know? And I know on every Saturday, my mother-- whenever she cooked, she cooked for them, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were living in the ghetto, what were you learning about the world, about what was going on outside of Nazi territory? Did you have a radio? Did you have access to any papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All the radios were confiscated the first moment they came in. We had no Jewish papers. We only know what we know what we find in German papers. Lots of Jewish people could read the German newspaper. So we got that kind of information. And you know, we also find out things. We still have contact with Poles. So the Poles didn't have any radios either. They took away from the Poles also the radios. But we would know what was going on. We would see, like in 1940, that he was attacking you the Western world-- that France was occupied, Belgium, Holland. I mean, that was very well-publicized. We were aware of everything that was going on without having the radios and without having the newspaper. Because we were not locked in. You see, we had contact with the Polish people. My family didn't have any contact. But others did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any knowledge of extermination camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, people came escaped from Konin to our town. And they claimed that they buried their parents in Chelmno. We hear those stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, nobody ever would want to believe those things. Nobody wants to even listen to them. Nobody wants to hear those stores. What do you mean they're going to take people? I said. And they tried to convince us that they buried their parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't want to believe them. We chased them out. That was the Jewish police-- they find out. They were looking for them. So they escaped. They went into another ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was too unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't want to believe it. We didn't want to hear those stories. Because we thought it was propaganda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what community did you say they came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Konin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Konin. K-O-N-I-N. Konin. This was not far from Chelmno. Chelmno was actually the camp what was the destruction-- the camp for destruction.

words.text: what was the destruction-- the camp

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lask ghetto, how did the deportations to the camps begin? What do you remember about people first being taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first transport went-- about 320 people. They went all to Poznan. In that vicinity there were transports going, that we really knew that there is a camp. We find out because we receive letters for certain vicinity around Poznan.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were still in the ghetto. We didn't even realize. I know I had a friend there. And he told me that-- he sent me a postal card that he looks like his uncle with the name. But his uncle was an old man. And I could not understand what he's telling me. What is he trying to tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, like he looks like Uncle [NON-ENGLISH]. And I know Uncle [NON-ENGLISH]. He was in the 60s. And in a way, 60s is an old man.

speaker: interviewee
text: What is he trying to tell me? We used to send him bread. We couldn't understand. We didn't realize what was going on in those camps. So even as the transports were beginning, you really did not realize what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did know that there was no good there. To which extent, we didn't. We couldn't. We couldn't imagine. You have to be there to see it to believe it, and what kind of condition. But it didn't take long until I was in a camp like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you taken? When were you taken from the ghetto to a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken by the end of March 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I was transported? I was picked up in the city. I was picked up with my brother Sam. And we were shipped away. They took us to the station. They shipped us. We didn't know in which direction we were going, where we were going. We wind up in a camp with the name Nekla, N-E-K-L-A. And this is also by Poznan. Now that was a part of the area that was going from Poznan to Konin and Kutno. One railroad was there-- one line. And they tried to put in a second line. So they put camps every seven kilometers or 12 kilometers. We were going to the left. We were going west or east or east or west. And this is our work what we were doing there. And this was in March-- in the end of March 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: So March 1942, you arrive at Nekla with your brother Sam. What was your first impression of that first concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not really a concentration camp. They used to call it a Jewish camp-- a Judenlager. When we came in, we find that people from another town were already there. They came in the morning. And we came in the afternoon. We find a gallows for four people to be hanged. And they said that you came in here to work. You can extend your life span by doing one thing. [GERMAN]-- that you have to work, work. And you've got to be obedient. And whoever's going to run away will be hanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you repeat that? You have to be a-- work, work, you have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] It just means you have to work, work, and you've got to be obedient.

speaker: interviewer
text: Obedient.

speaker: interviewee
text: Obedient. If not, this is what you're going to get. He pointed to the gallows. And they used it very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other forms of punishment you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the worst. Beating-- I mean, that didn't come into question. I mean, we got so accustomed to it that that wasn't a big deal, that somebody was beaten up. That was the worst camp I ever was, yes. We didn't even realize how bad it was. We had no sanitation facilities. We had no water. Within three, four weeks, people were dying like flies. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we had to work hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this camp located near a populated area at all? Were there non-Jews who could see what was going on or who had any knowledge of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That camp in Nekla was located near a station, a railroad station. But we were surrounded with farmland. We worked for them. They could see what was going on. And they were aware of it. Nobody would try to escape. Whoever tried to escape was caught, either by the Germans or by the Poles. And they were hanged, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] without being court martialed, believe me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Those were people that attempted escape. What about those who didn't? Was there talk of escape amongst the inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was no way to escape. Wherever you will escape, they would know. You could look at us. You could see we were undernourished.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were declothed. We hadn't got any clothes anymore. You know? They didn't change any-- nothing. Whatever we got, that's what we had to use. So we looked like a different element, like we had come from a different world. What clothing did you have? What were you given?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever we came in from Lask. Whatever we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. There was nothing handed out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They didn't give us any special uniform, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the guards at that camp? What were their nationalities?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Polish guards. They were not armed. And actually, if somebody wants to run away, he could have. But there is nowhere to run. You see? You were in Nekla like you would be in the center of Siberia. Wherever you're going to go, you're going to freeze to death. And this was exactly the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep? What were the barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were only three barracks there in that camp. We slept-- they put straw on one side of the barrack. And I would say they were about 60 feet long. They put it on one side of the barracks-- 60 by 20, or maybe even by 15 or 16. I don't even know. They put seven-foot or eight-foot boards that the straw shouldn't fall over. In the center was a table. And this was it. This was the living room, dining room, kitchen, and all the facilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many of you were in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were one next to the other. We were packed in next to the others. There were no bunks, nothing. On the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amongst the inmates, did anyone ever seem to take charge? Was anyone in charge? Did it seem like anyone took charge of the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: They organized who should be the Lagerfuhrer, who should be the leader. You know. They separated people to work. Those people remained at the camp. They didn't go to work. That was like the Lagerfuhrer, they used to call it-- Blockalteste. We had some from our own people that worked in the kitchens and in the yards in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that cause friction?

speaker: interviewee
text: That caused friction because some of them-- the Jewish people who had come earlier, they had the best positions. So they gave the best positions to their own. And we remained, actually, the last ones. We had the worst. We had to take whatever they gave us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked on the railroad for the railroad company.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any medical provisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely nothing. No medical facilities at all. Every so often they used to take-- they come in. They used to make selections. Whoever was not fit to work, they just took them away. And we know that they killed them all. They used to clean up lots of camps. They didn't just went to our camp. They took transports and they shipped them away. Some of them were shot right on the spot. And some of them they just shipped them to Chelmno. We know that that's where they're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this time you knew-- you were learning more of what was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh we knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew it. They didn't try to keep it a secret. And we were aware of it. We were aware of the full extent. Naturally, we did not know that the Final Solution was issued at the Wannsee Conference. We did not know. But we know that that-- I mean, the Jew hasn't got any value whatsoever. Anybody could do with them whatever they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Nekla?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came there by the end of March. And we remained in that camp-- I was with my brother Sam. And we were there until October, November, something in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: November of-- the year is--

speaker: interviewee
text: The year 1942. Then we came to a camp, Gutenbrunn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you arrive there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they took us by train. They put us in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the train like? What kind of a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cattle-- you know. Like a cattle train. It took only about a half an hour, 3/4 of an hour. And we were on the next-- we were shipped to Gutenbrunn. Over there, we find-- as we came in, we find a gallows a little bit larger than for four people, you see. And that was approximately under the same conditions that were in Nekla. Very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe that this was where they were going to keep you? Did you understand why you had been moved? Were you given any reason for being moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that the work at the railroad came to an end. What they did in the beginning-- let's say, for instance, if there were maybe 500 or 450 people, they need only half-- let's say 250. I remember they used to make a [GERMAN]. They used to call it a [GERMAN]. That means they took the people that were not needed. And they shipped them away. Now I told you that that was a big selection. That was also during in the summer 1942. They needed half of the camp. Now at that time, the leadership was done by the people from Wloclawek, from a city from-- from the different--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, W-L-O-C-L-A-K-- Wloclawek-- W-E-K, or whatever. They used to call it Hohensalza in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: They called it what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hohensalza. They changed the name, you know? But you see, they were the leaders in our camp. So they tried to protect their own people. Then there started coming friction. We find out. And we start arguing with them. Let the Germans pick whatever they want to. Let us-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the guilt fall on our hand. But for one or another reason, it looks like they couldn't come to an understanding.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had somebody from our town whose name was Grunner-- Josker Grunner, Joe Grunner. And he was working with the Germans even in Lask. And he knew how to go around. So he made a deal with the Lagerfuhrer, with the Germans. So the deal was made that the Germans will pick whatever they want to, and nobody should be picking. And also, those two-- there were two brothers, the leaders from the camp. They were also shipped away.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] was their name-- two brothers with the name [NON-ENGLISH]. And then Grunner actually took over the leadership. But you know, we did not pick any other-- we left the picking to the Germans. Let them to do whatever they want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was more arbitrary. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I remember like today, we had to go in front of a window. And inside were the Germans. We didn't even know. You have to tell your name. They marked it down. We didn't know who was going to go left and right and who was going to remain, and who's not going to remain. By the end, we find out that one of my friends-- David Leder. He lives in Israel-- that he is on the list to go actually away to Chelmno to be destroyed. So the only thing that we could have done is we gave him that he should read the list. When it's going to come, his name, what he has done is mention a name prior, that was there already. You know, what was, you know, that separated us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, this is the way he went by. His name was never mentioned. Because we gave him the list. That was the setup. You understand what I mean? Mhm. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived. He is still today in Jerusalem. He lives in Israel. Naturally, somebody else went  in his place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You say that Gutenbrunn was similar to Nekla. Was it similar in terms of the food? How was that handled. What did you receive?

speaker: interviewee
text: The work was the same. We worked at the railroad. In Nekla we were already accustomed to the people that we worked with. In here, we start now with new people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came into another camp. So actually, we were the outsiders. We were the newcomers. You understand what I mean? Naturally, it's not so good. Now if you've got newcomers, the situation changes for the people who are there in existence.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had a Lagerfuhrer. The only difference was that we did not have any lice anymore in Gutenbrunn. The facilities were there different.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have any more--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, in Nekla, that was impossible. It's unreal. Whoever was not in Nekla cannot imagine what Nekla meant. If somebody wants to imagine hell on this earth, that was this camp. We had no facilities. We had no clothing. We had nothing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I would say from our people in Nekla, before we came to Gutenbrunn, 50% were dead within the-- actually between April and October. I was also very, very sick. I was so sick that I couldn't walk. Like you see sometimes in the movies, you see that people are walking in the camps-- they could not walk straight. They walk on all fours, on their hands and legs. I was walking like that. And I was at that time-- in 1942, I was 19 years old. Pure luck saved me, that I recuperated before actually the big transport went where they picked the capable and incapable-- the people that were fit to work and people that were not fit to work. I was a healthy person at that time, when I needed. They picked me that I should remain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was pure luck. If this would be maybe a month earlier, I wouldn't be here to tell the story. You say--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a disease-- dysentery. People got sick in the morning sometimes, and in the afternoon, they were gone. People that went out to work healthy-- this attacked them during the work. They came back dead. They just brought them back dead. It was a terrible camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see what happened to the bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to bury them. We had a place. They picked a place in Nekla. You're talking about Nekla, right? Yeah. In the first camp. Believe it or not, I find a list. From every person that was buried, they had a list. And I got it in my possession. I showed it to my brother-- the list of what people were buried in Nekla-- so many of them. We were only-- actually, we were only about five months there. We were April, May, June, July, August, September, October.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they buried--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about seven months. Half of the people were gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they buried together? Who buried them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. That all depends. Every day they used to carry out people. 10 people, 20, five-- when there were two, they waited for the next day. But every second day-- every second day-- people were buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was responsible for doing the burying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, people that came home. They had an extra-- they get something extra to eat. They were doing it. You know, people were looking for that kind of work. Somebody had to do it. You couldn't stay with them. You could not have the dead bodies there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now after Nekla, you're at Gutenbrunn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gutenbrunn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gutenbrunn. Was the situation similar as far as the burial and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: The killings happened in the same way? Now the guards at Gutenbrunn, were they the same Pol--

speaker: interviewee
text: Similar. Similar.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same nationality? Again, did they have weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were not watched. Whoever wants to escape could have escaped. Whoever wants to organize could have organized. But if you were caught, you were hanged. I remember one episode. My brother Sam-- he lives now in Chicago. He tried to go for potatoes. And I have seen that he got two other guys. And they got little conferences, you know, what the plans are. But I also noticed that he had something behind his jacket. You know, like a bag of putting potatoes into. I said, you remain in here. You don't go with nobody. Sooner or later, you will be caught. We don't need potatoes. When they're gonna catch you you won't have any need for nothing anymore. So I tried to hold onto him, watch him, for about three or four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the other two guys didn't have patience to wait any longer. So they went on their own. They were caught. And they were hanged.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember we came home for lunch. And we have seen the bodies were laying. We had to go around the dead bodies. We have not seen the way they were hanged. They were dead already. And we had to go around them. And I said to my brother Sam, you would have been the third one in here if you wouldn't listen to me. He said, forget it. Don't mention it. I mean, that was pure luck. You know, we were together for so many years. He always listened to me. We never had an argument between each other. It's unreal. Because so many people-- fathers with sons. The father took away the bread from the son and the son from the father. We helped each other, always. I remember my mother used to tell me, watch over him. And the meaning-- the way she told me that I should watch over him. It was like she had a premonition. That, you know, he's got to be watched. Each mother knows her child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Done.
